title: Ad-hoc port selectors: parser
analysis:
This task adds parsing support for the ad-hoc port selector operator `@`, used as `expr @ ident(args)`. The operator is a postfix expression that takes a base expression, a selector name (identifier), and a parenthesized argument list. It should bind tighter than indexing (`[]`) but looser than member access (`.`).

The parser is tree-sitter based: grammar.js defines the CST, then ts_parser.rs lowers CST nodes to AST. The AST types live in lib.rs. Expression precedence is defined numerically in grammar.js (1=lowest to 8=highest for postfix). Currently member_access, function_call, and index_access all share precedence 8.

To implement the relative precedence (member_access > ad_hoc_selector > index_access), we adjust: member_access and function_call to prec 10, ad_hoc_selector to prec 9, index_access stays at 8. This preserves existing parse behavior for `a.b[0]` while correctly parsing `a.b @ face("top")[0]` as `((a.b) @ face("top"))[0]`.

The ExprKind enum in lib.rs needs a new `AdHocSelector` variant. Adding this variant will break exhaustive matches in downstream crates (notably compile_expr_guarded in reify-compiler). However, this task is scoped to crates/reify-syntax and tree-sitter-reify only. The compiler support is a separate task. The workspace check can be done with `--package reify-syntax` to verify parser correctness independently.

Review feedback identified a blocking issue: the AdHocSelector arm in compile_expr_guarded (lib.rs:1590) uses panic! instead of following the established error-handling pattern (push Diagnostic::error + return CompiledExpr::literal fallback). This must be fixed to prevent compiler crashes when users write @ expressions. Steps 10-11 address this with a TDD test-then-fix cycle.

prerequisites.description: Add `ad_hoc_selector` grammar rule to grammar.js: `expr '@' ident '(' args ')'` at prec.left(9). Bump member_access and function_call to prec 10 (from 8). Update precedence comment. Add `ad_hoc_selector` to `_expression` choice.
Regenerate tree-sitter parser: run `tree-sitter generate` in tree-sitter-reify/ to produce updated parser.c, grammar.json, and node-types.json.

Add tree-sitter corpus test file `test/corpus/ad_hoc_selector.txt` with basic parse cases: simple `a @ face("top")`, no-arg `a @ default()`, multi-arg `a @ point(x, y, z)`, chained with member access `a.b @ face("top")`.

steps.description: Write failing test `parse_ad_hoc_selector_basic` in ts_parser.rs: parse `structure S { let x = port @ face("top") }` and assert the let expr is `ExprKind::AdHocSelector { base: Ident("port"), selector: "face", args: [StringLiteral("top")] }`. This test will fail to compile because ExprKind::AdHocSelector does not exist yet.

Add `AdHocSelector { base: Box<Expr>, selector: String, args: Vec<Expr> }` variant to `ExprKind` in crates/reify-syntax/src/lib.rs. Test still fails because lowering is not implemented — parser returns None for ad_hoc_selector nodes.

Add lowering in ts_parser.rs: (1) Add `"ad_hoc_selector" => self.lower_ad_hoc_selector(node)` to `lower_expr` match. (2) Implement `lower_ad_hoc_selector` method that extracts field("base"), field("selector"), and argument_list children, lowering each. Test from step-1 should now pass.

Write test `parse_ad_hoc_selector_no_args` in the integration test file: parse `structure S { let x = port @ default() }` and assert `AdHocSelector { base: Ident("port"), selector: "default", args: [] }`. Should pass immediately.
Write test `parse_ad_hoc_selector_multiple_args`: parse `structure S { let x = port @ point(1, 2, 3) }` and assert `AdHocSelector` with 3 `NumberLiteral` args. Should pass immediately.

Write test `parse_ad_hoc_selector_chained_member_access`: parse `structure S { let x = sub_part.mount @ face("top") }` and assert `AdHocSelector { base: MemberAccess { object: Ident("sub_part"), member: "mount" }, ... }`. Verifies member access binds tighter than `@`.

Write test `parse_ad_hoc_selector_in_connect`: parse `structure S { port a : out T  port b : in T  connect a @ face("top") -> b @ face("bottom") }` and verify both connect port refs contain `AdHocSelector` nodes.

Write test `parse_ad_hoc_selector_precedence_over_index`: parse `structure S { let x = port @ face("top")[0] }` and assert the outer expression is `IndexAccess { object: AdHocSelector{...}, index: NumberLiteral(0) }`, verifying @ binds tighter than [].

Write test `parse_ad_hoc_selector_with_expr_args`: parse `structure S { let x = port @ edge(width * 2) }` and assert the arg is a BinOp expression, verifying complex expressions work in selector args.

Write test `compile_ad_hoc_selector_emits_diagnostic` in new file crates/reify-compiler/tests/ad_hoc_selector_compile_tests.rs. Use the `compile_module_with_diagnostics` pattern from purpose_compile_tests.rs: parse `structure S { let x = port @ face("top") }`, compile, assert no panic occurs, assert diagnostics contain a Severity::Error with message containing "ad-hoc selector (@) is not yet supported". This test will fail because the current code panics instead of emitting a diagnostic.

Fix the AdHocSelector arm in compile_expr_guarded (crates/reify-compiler/src/lib.rs:1588-1591). Replace `panic!("AdHocSelector is not yet supported in the compiler")` with the established pattern: push `Diagnostic::error("ad-hoc selector (@) is not yet supported in the compiler").with_label(DiagnosticLabel::new(expr.span, "not yet supported"))` to diagnostics, and return `CompiledExpr::literal(Value::Undef, Type::Real)`. Test from step-10 should now pass.

design_decisions.decision: Set ad_hoc_selector at tree-sitter prec.left(9), bump member_access and function_call to prec 10
design_decisions.rationale: The task requires @ to bind looser than member access but tighter than indexing. Currently all postfix ops share prec 8. By making member_access/function_call=10, ad_hoc_selector=9, index_access=8, the precedence ladder is explicit. prec.left ensures left-to-right associativity for `a.b @ sel()` → `(a.b) @ sel()`. Existing behavior is preserved since member_access > index_access was already true via left-associativity.

design_decisions.decision: Use standard argument_list (positional) for selector args, not named_argument_list
design_decisions.rationale: The syntax `expr @ ident(args)` uses the same positional argument list as function calls. Named arguments can be added later if needed. This mirrors the function_call rule which also uses $.argument_list.

design_decisions.decision: Scope this task to reify-syntax and tree-sitter-reify only, not touching the compiler
design_decisions.rationale: The task description specifies modules: crates/reify-syntax. Adding the AdHocSelector variant to ExprKind will cause a non-exhaustive match error in reify-compiler's compile_expr_guarded. However, the syntax crate can be checked independently with `cargo check -p reify-syntax`. Compiler support for the new variant is a separate task.

design_decisions.decision: Place tests in both ts_parser.rs (unit) and a new integration test file ad_hoc_selector_tests.rs
design_decisions.rationale: Following the project's test pattern: inline tests in ts_parser.rs for basic parsing validation using the parse_let_expr helper, and a dedicated integration test file for more complex scenarios like connect statements and precedence, matching the pattern of connect_chain_tests.rs, lambda_tests.rs, etc.

design_decisions.decision: Fix compiler panic in this parser task rather than deferring to a separate compiler task
design_decisions.rationale: Review identified the panic! in compile_expr_guarded as a blocking issue: it crashes the entire compiler process when any user source uses @ syntax. Since this task introduced the ExprKind::AdHocSelector variant and the panic! arm, the fix belongs here. The fix is minimal — replacing panic! with the established diagnostic+fallback pattern used by all other error paths in the same function — and does not implement actual compiler support for the operator.

reuse.how: Use for inline unit tests that parse `structure S { let x = <expr> }` and extract the ExprKind. Same pattern as index_access, member_access, and collection literal tests.

reuse.what: parse_decls helper pattern from integration tests
reuse.how: Copy the `parse_decls(source) -> (Vec<Declaration>, Vec<ParseError>)` helper for the new integration test file. This is the standard pattern used across all integration test files.
Reuse the existing $.argument_list rule for ad_hoc_selector args. This gives comma-separated positional expressions with optional trailing comma, identical to function_call syntax.
The lower_ad_hoc_selector method can follow the same pattern as lower_function_call for extracting positional arguments from an argument_list child node.
Follow the same format for the new corpus test: section headers with ===, source snippet, expected CST structure with ---.

reuse.what: compile_module_with_diagnostics helper and diagnostic assertion pattern
reuse.how: Copy the helper that compiles without asserting no errors, then filter diagnostics by Severity::Error and assert the expected message is present. Same pattern as compile_purpose_rejects_guarded_blocks test.
